contest.title: 2025-2026 ICPC, NERC, Northern Eurasia Finals (Unrated, Online Mirror, ICPC Rules, Teams Preferred)

sources.notes: Official NERC tutorial; used to confirm the two-state parity DP over adjacent x/y consistency.

reference_candidates.notes: 洛谷题解候选来源；只有实际参考后才移动到 sources。

annotation.summary: Given two binary strings with possible mistakes, change the fewest characters so that the second string matches the running parity relation induced by the first.
annotation.constraints: The strings are long, but the consistency relation only depends on adjacent parity, so a two-state DP suffices.
annotation.core_idea: Let the corrected medical parity string be y. The corrected x_i is forced by consecutive y values: x_i = y_{i-1} xor y_i, with y_0 fixed. Therefore choosing the whole corrected y sequence determines x. Run a DP over the current value y_i; transition from previous bit p to current bit q costs one if x_i differs from the input x_i plus one if q differs from the input y_i. This is a standard finite-state DP on a binary automaton.
annotation.complexity: O(n) per test case with two states.
tricks: represent x by adjacent parity differences of y

solution_variants.name: main
solution_variants.summary: Choose each corrected y_i by DP; the previous y bit and current y bit determine the corrected x_i and the local edit cost.

tags.evidence: Only the previous corrected y bit is needed as state, giving a constant-state automaton DP.
tags.description: Dynamic programming over a small finite set of states, often equivalent to a hand-built automaton.
tags.created_reason: This distinguishes constant-state recurrence problems from broad dp/string tags.

tags.evidence: The consistency relation is exactly an XOR/parity relation between adjacent corrected y values and x_i.
The input objects are binary strings and the cost is Hamming edits over their characters.
The first string is treated as the adjacent XOR difference sequence of the second string.
